topic: Someone's childhood imaginary friend appears in their niece's drawings
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the stack of crayon drawings spread across the kitchen table, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead mirroring the dull throb behind her eyes. Her niece, Lily, had been so cheerful this week, filling the paper with vibrant, nonsensical creatures. But underneath the smiling sun and the wobbly, rainbow-colored blobs, there was always that shadow—the spectral outline of Barnaby. Barnaby, the imaginary companion from her own childhood, the one who always knew the secrets she didn't want anyone else to know. As Eleanor picked up a drawing of a winged, purple beast, a cold knot tightened in her stomach. It wasn't just a drawing; it felt like an intrusion, a ghost from a time when she felt entirely alone, now being re-enacted in this innocent, childlike medium. She pushed the paper away, the scrape of the cardboard against the wood sounding unnecessarily loud in the quiet afternoon.

[story 2]

"Look, Aunt Clara! Barnaby is flying the kite!" Lily chirped, holding up a crayon rendition of a grinning, vaguely anthropomorphic shadow. Clara felt a tightening in her chest, a familiar clenching around her ribs that made her want to physically shrink into the armchair. She watched the drawing, and the familiar, hollow ache settled deep in her bones. It was the same figure from her youth, the one who existed only in the periphery of her memory, now materialized in her niece's clumsy artistic expression. Clara didn't look at the drawing for long. Her gaze flickered over the edges, cataloging the imperfections, the way the crayon bled slightly into the paper. A bitter taste rose in her mouth. Why did these things always surface when she was supposed to be enjoying this time?

[story 3]

I traced the outline of the creature in the sketchbook with a fingertip, the paper cool beneath my touch. It was him. That grinning, slightly distorted shape from when I was small and convinced the shadows held company. Now, it was Lily’s creation, innocent and bright, yet it carried the unmistakable echo of my own solitary landscape. A tightness gripped my jaw. I wanted to crumple the page, to erase the evidence that this spectral presence had somehow slipped past the boundaries of my own mind and manifested in this saccharine art. I sighed, a long, slow expulsion of air that seemed to carry all the accumulated weight of unspoken things. It was just a drawing, she told me, but to me, it was a stark reminder of a past I was trying very hard to leave behind.
